briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, creek channel, riprap. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: For the shallow zone in cold water, use a flatside crankbait (Hardliner). Extremely tight action — bass use their lateral line to hunt in cold water and flatsides displace a ton of water. Small p
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, creek channel, riprap. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Wor...

Palaniuk: 'The spot had a riprap bank with a steep drop, then a trough that came up onto a hard bottom ridge, tapered back off into another trough. The trough made a horseshoe. A 40-yard section where the fish wanted to live, with a 25-foot hole t...

Robertson: Spring crankbait progression -- 1) Prespawn: slow-roll lipless on flats near channel banks in 1-6 feet; 2) Postspawn: big-bodied squarebill on tapering points near spawning flats in under 10 feet; 3) Late postspawn: deep diver following...

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal pre-spawn bass are aggressive and moving shallow. Expect fish in the 4-8ft range on secondary points and channel swings. Falling pressure pulls fish up from staging areas — target the first breakline off spawning flats.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Rig up a Medium Diving Crankbait — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Deep Diving Crankbait — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Deep Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Robertson: When the swimbait bite slows and fish scatter, pick up a deep-diving crankbait like the SPRO Little John DD 70 to put the majority of your weight in the boat. It produced the bulk of a 32-pound bag.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish. Downsize to 1/4oz on ultra-shallow flats (1-2ft). Upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the fall and

briefing.headline: Rig up a Suspending Jerkbait — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Suspending Jerkbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: When water is 55 degrees or colder in pre-spawn, a Rapala Shad Rap is hard to beat for both numbers and quality of fish; targets deeper staging bass still off the bank.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: For a spring pre-spawn event limited to three rods, picks a jerkbait/crankbait for deeper staging fish, a 1/2-oz jig for versatility, and a ChatterBait for stained shallow water.

KVD: Pre-spawn one-two punch strategy: shallow crankbait or jerkbait on deeper edges outside pockets, then Thunder Cricket and Hardliner back in the bays where fish are moving to spawn. The combo covers both staging and migrating fish.

Palaniuk: 'If fish are high in the water column, pick up a jerkbait. If they're toward the bottom, pick up a crankbait. Use forward-facing sonar to see where fish are positioned and choose accordingly.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Ned Rig — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Ned Rig is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, they'll bite the Ned Rig on the fall. Cast out, let it sink on dead slack line — don't tighten up until it hits bottom.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Neko Rig — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Neko Rig is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.gameplan:
The Walking Topwater is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Use monofilament (Sufix Siege) for topwater fishing — the stretch prevents jerking the bait away from the fish on the hookset. Pull into the fish rather than jerking, letting the sharp VMC hooks get into the fish

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — steady pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — steady pre-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive pre-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for pre-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady pre-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

The Neko Rig is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: Use a Neko rig around docks when primary offshore spots get pressured — dock fish are often less beat up and can save a tournament late in the day

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Use a Neko rig around docks when primary offshore spots get pressured — dock fish are often less beat up and can save a tournament late in the day
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.

Yamamoto: The Chicken Rig is weedless unlike the Neko rig so it can be fished through brush, rocks, and docks. It has big bass appeal with the large Kut Tail profile. It's durable because the worm slides up the line on hookset

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable pre-spawn conditions mean fish are staging predictably at 5-12ft on points, channel swings, and riprap banks. They're feeding aggressively as water warms. Work the transition zone between deep winter haunts and shallow spawning areas.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If you get followers without commits, add scent or downsize your hook/weight

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady pre-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — finesse pre-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal pre-spawn fish pull back to 6-12ft staging areas. They won't commit shallow after the front. Target the first breakline off spawning flats and key on deeper structure near shallow spawning zones.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Rig up a Medium Diving Crankbait — finesse pre-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Deep Diving Crankbait — finesse pre-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Deep Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Robertson: When the swimbait bite slows and fish scatter, pick up a deep-diving crankbait like the SPRO Little John DD 70 to put the majority of your weight in the boat. It produced the bulk of a 32-pound bag.

The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish. The Spinnerbait is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — finesse pre-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Rigs a curly-tail worm (cut paddle tail) on a Carolina rig with 3/4-oz egg sinker, 22-inch fluorocarbon leader (range 18-26 inches), and 3/0 worm hook; retrieves with standard pull-and-drag devoid of deadstick pauses.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Flipping Jig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, dock, laydown. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Johnston: Don't soak a jig. The more flips you can get in, the more fish you get in front of, the more bites you will get. If they aren't going to eat on the first couple of hops, they can starve.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, dock, laydown. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Football Jig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Football Jig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Hackney: 'Use a 3/4 to 1-ounce football head jig on 12-17 pound fluorocarbon to cover water and locate sweet spots along gravel bars or ridges with isolated stumps. Same slow presentation as a Carolina rig but only one knot.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Texas Rig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Rigs a curly-tail worm (cut paddle tail) on a Carolina rig with 3/4-oz egg sinker, 22-inch fluorocarbon leader (range 18-26 inches), and 3/0 worm hook; retrieves with standard pull-and-drag devoid of deadstick pauses.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

Hackney: 'At Stick Marsh with no underwater vegetation, fish were spawning around willow trees and cane on the shoreline - they just wanted to have something to spawn around.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a 10" Worm — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The 10" Worm is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

For Yamamoto 10-inch grub: use Gamakatsu EWG Superline 5/0 hook with screw-in 1/4oz weight (F rig) and delayed hookset. Straight shank hooks cause missed fish

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Ned Rig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Ned Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, they'll bite the Ned Rig on the fall. Cast out, let it sink on dead slack line — don't tighten up until it hits bottom.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Neko Rig — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Neko Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Walking Topwater — aggressive spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Walking Topwater is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Use monofilament (Sufix Siege) for topwater fishing — the stretch prevents jerking the bait away from the fish on the hookset. Pull into the fish rather than jerking, letting the sharp VMC hooks get into the fish

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — steady spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Medium Diving Crankbait — steady spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Deep Diving Crankbait — steady spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Deep Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Robertson: When the swimbait bite slows and fish scatter, pick up a deep-diving crankbait like the SPRO Little John DD 70 to put the majority of your weight in the boat. It produced the bulk of a 32-pound bag.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — steady spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — finesse spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Rigs a curly-tail worm (cut paddle tail) on a Carolina rig with 3/4-oz egg sinker, 22-inch fluorocarbon leader (range 18-26 inches), and 3/0 worm hook; retrieves with standard pull-and-drag devoid of deadstick pauses.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — finesse spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Neko Rig — finesse spawn approach for clear water.

The Neko Rig is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a Chatterbait — aggressive spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Chatterbait is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Use a minnow-profile trailer (like CrushCity Freeloader) for natural, erratic movement - it adds realism and bulk without impeding the natural balsa-like hunting action of the ChatterBait.

The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:

briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — finesse spawn approach for stained water.

briefing.gameplan:

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Swim Jig — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Swim Jig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better act
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Stop the bait mid-cast and let it fall back toward bottom to increase catch rate by 20-30% — trailing bass can't resist a shad imitator dropping from the school like a dying shad
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Wheeler: The Punch Out Craw has five manifestations - full glide bait on 4/0 hook and 1/2-oz sinker, separated claws for football jig or Biffle Hardhead, trimmed small craw for swim jig/flipping jig/chatterbait/shakyhead, shortened torso for even ...
Yamamoto pro Hite: Standard Zako for ChatterBaits (subtle natural action), Kickin' Zako (paddle tail) for deep heavy ChatterBaits on ledges with poor light penetration and for swim jigs exclusively

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal spawning bass are active on beds in 2-5ft. Falling pressure often triggers new waves of fish to move onto beds. Target hard-bottom spawning flats, secondary pockets, and protected coves.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If moving baits aren't getting bit, slow down to a crawling retrieve or switch to a jig

briefing.headline: Rig up a Flipping Jig — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, dock, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Johnston: Don't soak a jig. The more flips you can get in, the more fish you get in front of, the more bites you will get. If they aren't going to eat on the first couple of hops, they can starve.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, dock, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Best for pitching to isolated cover - docks, logs, laydowns, boulders, stumps. A little tough to sink into thick cover. Effective on both smallmouth and largemouth, spawning, cruising, and fry guarding fish

Johnston: When a main pattern starts dwindling, have a backup plan ready. At St. Johns, flipping pads on the main river when the bed fish pattern faded was probably the best decision I could have made — it produced my biggest fish.

If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures

briefing.headline: Rig up a Football Jig — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Football Jig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Hackney: 'Use a 3/4 to 1-ounce football head jig on 12-17 pound fluorocarbon to cover water and locate sweet spots along gravel bars or ridges with isolated stumps. Same slow presentation as a Carolina rig but only one knot.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Texas Rig — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, cover. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, cover. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Hackney: 'Once water hits the 70s, fish pay more attention to baits with appendages that swim. Use something like a Game Hawg or Rage Bug. Fish grab it even when reeling back in.'

KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmo...

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Rigs a curly-tail worm (cut paddle tail) on a Carolina rig with 3/4-oz egg sinker, 22-inch fluorocarbon leader (range 18-26 inches), and 3/0 worm hook; retrieves with standard pull-and-drag devoid of deadstick pauses.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Yamamoto pro Yelas: 'Right around the spawn and after, the fish seem to prefer a smaller bait with less action' — downsize from the Cowboy during spawn and post-spawn
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Wheeler: Carolina rig setup uses 7.5-foot medium-heavy rod, 7.0:1 reel, 17-lb fluorocarbon mainline to barrel swivel, then 14-lb fluorocarbon leader of 22 inches (18-26 inch range).
Hackney: 'At Stick Marsh with no underwater vegetation, fish were spawning around willow trees and cane on the shoreline - they just wanted to have something to spawn around.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a 10" Worm — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The 10" Worm is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

For Yamamoto 10-inch grub: use Gamakatsu EWG Superline 5/0 hook with screw-in 1/4oz weight (F rig) and delayed hookset. Straight shank hooks cause missed fish

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

The Ned Rig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Switch to Ned rig (1/4-oz head, same baits as dropshot) when smallmouth are going down and nipping the dropshot weight instead of the bait

briefing.gameplan:
The Neko Rig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Use a Neko rig around docks when primary offshore spots get pressured — dock fish are often less beat up and can save a tournament late in the day

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — steady spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, riprap. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: The squarebill has a nice wide wobble and good vibration; bang it into stumps, rocks, and anything in front of you to get shallow fish to react.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, riprap. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Wor...

Robertson: Spring crankbait progression -- 1) Prespawn: slow-roll lipless on flats near channel banks in 1-6 feet; 2) Postspawn: big-bodied squarebill on tapering points near spawning flats in under 10 feet; 3) Late postspawn: deep diver following...

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable spawn conditions have fish committed to beds in 1-5ft. Focus on hard-bottom areas in protected coves, pockets, and flats. Polarized glasses are essential for sight-fishing.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Rig up a Medium Diving Crankbait — steady spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Deep Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Robertson: When the swimbait bite slows and fish scatter, pick up a deep-diving crankbait like the SPRO Little John DD 70 to put the majority of your weight in the boat. It produced the bulk of a 32-pound bag.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — steady spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — finesse spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: 'On shallow flats, use your rod tip and line size (10-20lb) to control depth. I want the bait hitting bottom. Every time it ticks cover, pop it and let it flutter — that's when fish bite.'

The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse spawn approach for muddy water.

The Shakyhead is a strong choice for spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Hackney: 'Go to a 1/0 hook on the 2.5 to deaden the action. A shad does not have aggressive action. You still get the big profile but more natural movement for pressured fish.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal post-spawn bass are transitioning and aggressive. Males guard fry in 2-6ft while females roam 5-12ft feeding heavily. Target both shallow fry-guarders and roaming post-spawn females near points.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Texas Rig — aggressive post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — aggressive post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Neko Rig — aggressive post-spawn approach for clear water.

The Neko Rig is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — steady post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Hackney: 'Go to a 1/0 hook on the 2.5 to deaden the action. A shad does not have aggressive action. You still get the big profile but more natural movement for pressured fish.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable post-spawn conditions split fish between shallow fry-guarders (2-5ft) and transitioning females (5-15ft). Shad spawn activity on seawalls, riprap, and bluff walls concentrates bigger fish shallow at dawn.

If you get followers without commits, add scent or downsize your hook/weight

briefing.headline: Rig up a Walking Topwater — steady post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Walking Topwater is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: Use monofilament (Sufix Siege) for topwater fishing — the stretch prevents jerking the bait away from the fish on the hookset. Pull into the fish rather than jerking, letting the sharp VMC hooks get into the fish

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse post-spawn approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Chatterbait — aggressive post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Chatterbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Use a minnow-profile trailer (like CrushCity Freeloader) for natural, erratic movement - it adds realism and bulk without impeding the natural balsa-like hunting action of the ChatterBait.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Swim Jig — aggressive post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Swim Jig is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — steady post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Pick a crankbait that goes deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel at a pace where you keep it in control. briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — steady post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. On heavier heads (3/8-1/2 oz) keep the tail tabs intact as they won't affect action with the heavier weight

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Flipping Jig — finesse post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse post-spawn approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Pick a crankbait that goes deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel at a pace where you keep it in control. briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal post-spawn bass are transitioning and aggressive. Males guard fry in 2-6ft while females roam 5-12ft feeding heavily. Target both shallow fry-guarders and roaming post-spawn females near points.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: 'On shallow flats, use your rod tip and line size (10-20lb) to control depth. I want the bait hitting bottom. Every time it ticks cover, pop it and let it flutter — that's when fish bite.'

briefing.gameplan:

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a 10" Worm — steady post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The 10" Worm is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — steady post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a Flipping Jig — finesse post-spawn approach for muddy water.

The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse post-spawn approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Hackney: 'Go to a 1/0 hook on the 2.5 to deaden the action. A shad does not have aggressive action. You still get the big profile but more natural movement for pressured fish.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Wor...

Robertson: Spring crankbait progression -- 1) Prespawn: slow-roll lipless on flats near channel banks in 1-6 feet; 2) Postspawn: big-bodied squarebill on tapering points near spawning flats in under 10 feet; 3) Late postspawn: deep diver following...
Wheeler: Best offshore spots included areas adjacent to grass lines, actual holes in the grass, and hard-bottomed shell beds in 8-35 feet of water.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a mix of rock, sand, and weed in 15 feet or less. Hit as many spots as you can and hope the right ones bite.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal summer bass feed aggressively. Fish split between shallow morning patterns (2-6ft) and offshore structure (12-25ft). Target grass lines, points, and ledges as falling pressure triggers movement.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.gameplan:
The Suspending Jerkbait is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: Even in slick calm clear conditions, a jerkbait over the top of them will draw fish from distance. 'They're going to see it and they're going to come.' Don't switch to finesse just because it's calm.

briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

briefing.gameplan:

Johnston: Use big baits on a Carolina Rig for bigger fish offshore — 10-inch worms, magnum flukes, and full-sized creature baits. Fish it on the same hard spots as a football jig. Use an 8.1:1 reel to pick up slack quickly and a heavy flipping rod.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Walking Topwater — aggressive summer approach for clear water.

The Walking Topwater is a strong choice for summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Used an X-Rap Prop and handcarved prop bait in bluegill patterns around bluegill beds; when a bass dogs the topwater darting back and forth without striking, pause the bait for as long as 6 seconds until the fish inhales it.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with clear water and stable conditions.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — steady summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and stable conditions.

Johnston: Use big baits on a Carolina Rig for bigger fish offshore — 10-inch worms, magnum flukes, and full-sized creature baits. Fish it on the same hard spots as a football jig. Use an 8.1:1 reel to pick up slack quickly and a heavy flipping rod.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — steady summer approach for clear water.

The Drop Shot is a strong choice for summer with clear water and stable conditions.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish an 80-yard stretch of bottom in about 40 feet targeting a pod of giant smallmouth. Catch one, then leave the spot and make a big run away before returning, because pressured deepwater smallmouth shut down after each catch.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with clear water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Flipping Jig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Texas Rig — finesse summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Texas Rig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Wheeler: In mornings bass sit looser to the outside of cover; as the sun gets up they move further into the cover, requiring a toad early and flipping deeper in the cover later.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — finesse summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use big baits on a Carolina Rig for bigger fish offshore — 10-inch worms, magnum flukes, and full-sized creature baits. Fish it on the same hard spots as a football jig. Use an 8.1:1 reel to pick up slack quickly and a heavy flipping rod.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — finesse summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish an 80-yard stretch of bottom in about 40 feet targeting a pod of giant smallmouth. Catch one, then leave the spot and make a big run away before returning, because pressured deepwater smallmouth shut down after each catch.

The Neko Rig is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

KVD: In clear water, use dice/bug baits (Sexy Bug, Tumbleweed) on a Neko rig — incredible action and visibility. In stained water, stick to the traditional Neko worm for a subtler profile. Superglue the tungsten weights so they don't fly out when fish jump.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — finesse summer approach for clear water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with clear water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — aggressive summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Pick a crankbait that goes deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel at a pace where you keep it in control. briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

Palaniuk: 'Deep diving crankbaits are really one of the few ways we have of generating any kind of reaction strike through much of the year on tour.'

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — aggressive summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Walking Topwater — aggressive summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Walking Topwater is a strong choice for summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions.

Wheeler: Used an X-Rap Prop and handcarved prop bait in bluegill patterns around bluegill beds; when a bass dogs the topwater darting back and forth without striking, pause the bait for as long as 6 seconds until the fish inhales it.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Rig up a 10" Worm — steady summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The 10" Worm is a strong choice for summer with stained water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: An old-school TX-rigged 6-inch worm on ledges is something people forget about — you can go behind other anglers and get bites when fish won't eat a crankbait or are not grouped up. Use 3/8-oz tungsten, 4/0 hook, 14-lb fluorocarbon

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Drop Shot — steady summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Drop Shot is a strong choice for summer with stained water and stable conditions.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish an 80-yard stretch of bottom in about 40 feet targeting a pod of giant smallmouth. Catch one, then leave the spot and make a big run away before returning, because pressured deepwater smallmouth shut down after each catch.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with stained water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Squarebill Crankbait — finesse summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Squarebill Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with stained water and post frontal conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Pick a crankbait that goes deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel at a pace where you keep it in control. When it hits a rock, try to get it to crawl up and over and then back down the other side -- looks like a crawfish slowly scooting, acts like something between a normal crankbait 

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Lipless Crankbait — aggressive summer approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Lipless Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with muddy water and pre frontal conditions.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.gameplan:

briefing.headline: Rig up a Shakyhead — steady summer approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Shakyhead is a strong choice for summer with muddy water and stable conditions.

Palaniuk: 'Extremely cold (42 degrees or below) and warmer than 90 degrees are prime conditions for a shaky-head. At cold extremes, fish are just up shallow beginning to get aggressive but not ready to eat a jig. A shaky-head is a small weedless finesse bait that's easy for lethargic fish to eat.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Rig up a Carolina Rig — finesse summer approach for muddy water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Carolina Rig is a strong choice for summer with muddy water and post frontal conditions.

Johnston: Use big baits on a Carolina Rig for bigger fish offshore — 10-inch worms, magnum flukes, and full-sized creature baits. Fish it on the same hard spots as a football jig. Use an 8.1:1 reel to pick up slack quickly and a heavy flipping rod.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.
